content: Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) is atherosclerotic occlusion of lower extremity arteries, affecting ~8.5 million Americans ≥40 years. Pathophysiology identical to coronary/cerebrovascular atherosclerosis—endothelial dysfunction, plaque formation, stenosis/occlusion. Risk factors: smoking (strongest association), diabetes (increases risk 2-4x, accelerates progression), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, age, CKD. Clinical presentations: asymptomatic (most common), intermittent claudication (exertional leg pain relieved by rest), atypical ischemic leg pain, critical limb ischemia (rest pain, tissue loss, gangrene), acute limb ischemia (sudden onset—embolism or thrombosis). Functional impairment significant even in asymptomatic patients. PAD is marker for systemic atherosclerosis—high risk of MI, stroke, CV death. Ankle-brachial index (ABI): ratio of systolic BP at ankle to arm; normal 1.00-1.40, borderline 0.91-0.99, abnormal ≤0.90 (diagnostic), noncompressible >1.40 (calcified vessels—common in diabetes, CKD).
